description: The module can operate as a PCIe Root Complex to enumerate and manage downstream endpoints. Software selects RC mode using device-level mode bits before link bring-up. Initialization includes enabling SerDes PLL, powering the module, disabling LTSSM for configuration, and then enabling LTSSM for training. Successful training is verified via the LTSSM state in a debug register before bus enumeration proceeds. Post-training, system software performs enumeration, config-space setup, and remote device initialization.
evidence: Section 2.11.1.1 Initialization Sequence: steps 3, 6, 8, 9, 10–12; Registers: DEVSTAT[PCIESSMODE], CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN], DEBUG0[LTSSM_STATE]
dependencies: SerDes reference clock and PLL locked; PCIe power/clock domain enabled; DEVSTAT mode set to RC
Remarks: Ensure DEVSTAT PCIESSMODE is set prior to enabling LTSSM; verify DEBUG0 before enumeration.

description: The controller supports endpoint operation with dedicated endpoint interrupt registers. Endpoint-specific status and clear registers allow local event signaling and integration with host-generated MSI. Endpoint operation follows the general operations framework described for the module. Configuration and interrupt handling are exposed through memory-mapped registers. Software orchestrates BAR setup and inbound translation to present device address space to the host.
evidence: Registers: 3.1.18 EP_IRQ_CLR, 3.1.19 EP_IRQ_STATUS; Chapter 2 Operations (2.11) context
dependencies: Proper BAR configuration and inbound translation rules; Link trained and enabled
Remarks: Map BARs and inbound regions before exposing endpoint functions; clear EP interrupts via EP_IRQ_CLR.

feature_name: SerDes PLL and reference clock configuration
description: The PCIe SerDes requires a low-jitter reference clock and PLL configuration prior to link bring-up. The PLL supports selectable bandwidth modes to balance jitter transfer and loop noise depending on clock quality. Control bits include SLEEPPLL, VRANGE, and DIVCLK enable, with lock status available in a SerDes status register. Software must program PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL and wait for LOCK before enabling LTSSM. Improper PLL settings may prevent link training and standards compliance.
evidence: Section 2.3 SerDes Configuration: PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL fields (SLEEPPLL, VRANGE, bandwidth selection) and PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK]
dependencies: Reference clock present; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL programmed; PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK]=1
Remarks: Choose bandwidth per clocking topology (direct vs cleaner PLL); keep PLL asleep only when link is intentionally down.

description: Link training is controlled by the LTSSM enable bit and verified via a debug state field. Software disables LTSSM during configuration, then asserts it to start training. Completion is detected when the LTSSM state transitions to the operational code. This gating ensures deterministic setup of BARs and translation before traffic. Failure to meet prerequisites can stall training or cause unstable links.
dependencies: SerDes PLL locked; power/clock domains active; correct mode selected
Remarks: Gate LTSSM until configuration writes complete; poll DEBUG0 for 0x11 before enumeration.

feature_name: Force link state and PHY loopback for test
description: The controller provides a Port Force Link register to bypass normal Detect and drive the LTSSM into a specified state for test. For PHY loopback, software configures SerDes lane RX settings, forces POLL_ACTIVE via PL_FORCE_LINK, and observes training completion. This aids validation without a far-end partner. Forced states must be cleared to resume compliant operation. Using these controls accelerates bring-up diagnostics and margining.
evidence: Section describing PHY loopback and PL_FORCE_LINK usage (2.11.1.1 related notes); Register: PL_FORCE_LINK[FORCE_LINK, LINK_STATE]
dependencies: SerDes configured; LTSSM force fields set; test mode enabled as required
Remarks: Only use forced states and loopback in lab; restore normal LTSSM flow for production.

description: The PCIe link supports autonomous low-power transitions L0s and L1 under ASPM control. Transitions are enabled through configuration space and managed by hardware once enabled. L3 (power-off) is inherently supported at system level; L2 is not supported by this module. Proper ASPM tuning improves idle power without impacting performance when configured correctly. Software should enable only modes validated for the platform’s latency budget.
evidence: Section 2.15.2 Link State Power Management: L0, L0s, L1 supported; L2 not supported; L3 power-off
dependencies: Link trained; configuration space ASPM controls set appropriately
Remarks: Coordinate ASPM with system policy and downstream device capabilities to avoid unintended link throttling.

feature_name: Reset mechanisms: cold, warm, and software hot reset
description: The module supports power-on (cold), system warm (hard/soft), and software-initiated hot reset. A software reset bit (INIT_RST) in RSTCMD issues a downstream-propagating hot reset, typically by a root complex. After any reset, registers return to defaults except sticky bits, and software must observe a minimum 100 ms quiet period before transactions. If a device does not respond, timeouts should extend to 1.5 seconds as guidance. Power domain transitions may require a full power-on reset to exit certain states.
evidence: Section 2.13 Reset Considerations: RSTCMD[INIT_RST]; timing notes (100 ms, up to 1.5 s); sticky bit behavior
dependencies: Appropriate reset source asserted; power domains sequenced; observe timing requirements
Remarks: Use hot reset only from RC; ensure software reinitializes config after warm/soft resets respecting sticky fields.

description: Inbound address translation maps PCIe BAR-targeted addresses to internal bus addresses. Four region-specific register sets define BAR match, PCIe start address (low/high for 32/64-bit), and internal offset. Each region activates when incoming TLPs hit the programmed BAR and range. This enables flexible placement of endpoint-visible memory windows. Correct 32-bit vs 64-bit programming of START_HI is essential.
evidence: Section 2 Inbound translator overview; Registers: IB_BARn, IB_STARTn_LO/HI, IB_OFFSETn (e.g., 3.1.82–3.1.97)
dependencies: BARs configured; inbound regions enabled with correct base/size; 32/64-bit fields consistent
Remarks: Align regions to power-of-two sizes for mask-based decoding; ensure IB_START matches BAR for deterministic mapping.

feature_name: Outbound Address Translation Unit (ATU) — up to 32 regions
description: Outbound translation provides multiple regions to map internal addresses to PCIe address space. Each region has high offset fields to support 64-bit addressing and an enable bit per region. Software selects and enables regions to generate outbound memory requests into host/device memory. This mechanism supports scatter windows for DMA-like access patterns even without a dedicated DMA engine. Disable unused regions to minimize decode complexity.
evidence: Registers: OB_ENABLEn (Table 3-80), OB_OFFSETn_HI (Table 3-81); N=0..31 noted in field descriptions
dependencies: Internal masters configured; regions enabled with proper offsets; link active
Remarks: Group related buffers per-region to simplify doorbell/completion routing; verify no overlap across active regions.

feature_name: BARs and masks with Memory/I-O and 64-bit support
description: The module implements standard PCIe Base Address Registers with mask registers for sizing. BAR fields select Memory or I/O type, with 64-bit BAR realized via BAR0/BAR1 pairing when configured. Mask registers aid software in determining and programming aperture size. Correct BAR programming underpins inbound translation and host discovery. I/O BAR usage should be limited to legacy systems; prefer Memory BARs for throughput.
evidence: Registers: 3.4.3 BAR0, 3.4.4 BAR0_MASK, 3.4.5 BAR1, 3.4.6 BAR1_MASK; Field refs Table 3-117 and 3-121 (0=Memory,1=I/O); 3.4.7–3.4.8 64-bit BAR
dependencies: Enumeration phase sizing; BAR type selection; alignment to mask-derived size
Remarks: Perform standard BAR sizing write/read during enumeration; for 64-bit BARs program both low/high dwords.

feature_name: MSI interrupt support with grouped vector registers
description: Message Signaled Interrupts are supported with raw status, enabled status (W1C), and enable set/clear registers. Vectors are grouped across multiple MSIx-like banks (e.g., MSI0..MSI6) with each bit corresponding to specific vector numbers. Software enables, services, and clears interrupts using these registers. Raw status can be used for debug injection where supported. Proper masking prevents spurious service during initialization.
dependencies: MSI capability enabled in config space; vector enables programmed; ISR clears W1C status
Remarks: Map MSI vectors to system interrupt controller; defer enabling until handlers and BARs are set.

description: Endpoint-specific interrupt status and clear registers provide fine-grained local event reporting. Software reads EP_IRQ_STATUS to identify active conditions and writes EP_IRQ_CLR to clear serviced events. This mechanism complements MSI delivery to a host by maintaining local source accounting. Using both ensures robust interrupt handling under high event rates. Proper ordering prevents lost events across clock domains.
dependencies: Clock domain active; interrupt sources configured; MSI delivery path operational (if used)
Remarks: Read-modify-clear not required when using dedicated clear register; batch clears per event class.

description: The controller supports 64-bit addressing in both inbound and outbound paths. Inbound translator START_HI fields and outbound high offset registers enable mapping above 4 GB. 64-bit BAR configurations allow the device to expose large memory apertures to a host. Software must consistently program high and low parts to avoid split decoding. This capability is essential for high-capacity buffers and 64-bit systems.
dependencies: PCIe link up; consistent 64-bit address programming across related registers
Remarks: Validate host support for 64-bit memory space; fall back to 32-bit mapping if required.

feature_name: Power and clock domain control for PCIe module
description: Before initialization, the PCIe module’s power and clock domains must be enabled at the device level. Enabling can be done via device control (e.g., PCIESSEN) or power/sleep controller as documented in device manuals. This step precedes SerDes PLL programming and LTSSM enable. Skipping it results in inaccessible registers and failed link bring-up. Proper sequencing ensures stable operation across resets.
evidence: Section 2.11.1.1 step 2 (enable power/clock domain via device-level control); device-level DEVSTAT PCIESSEN reference
dependencies: System PSC/clock control configured; PCIESSEN=1 prior to register access
Remarks: Gate clocks only after link is quiesced; coordinate with reset scheme to avoid partial resets.

description: A standard Power Management capability is implemented in configuration space. Fields include capability ID, next capability pointer (by default pointing to MSI structure), spec version, and device-specific initialization flags. This enables OS-directed power management and D-states handling. PME clock is hardwired as indicated. Proper capability chaining allows discovery by software enumerators.
evidence: Registers: Power Management Capability (Table 3-150): PM_CAP_ID, PM_NEXT_PTR (points to MSI), PME_SPEC_VER, DSI_N, AUX_CURR_N
dependencies: Configuration space accessible; capability list walking enabled
Remarks: Keep capability pointers consistent when updating config templates; advertise only supported PM states.

description: Standard bridge-related registers exist for bus numbering, IO/memory base/limit, and secondary status. These are used in RC/bridge contexts to define downstream resource ranges. Correct programming enables address decoding for subordinate buses. Misconfiguration can lead to decoding gaps or overlaps. These registers form the backbone for hierarchical PCIe resource allocation.
dependencies: RC/bridge mode selected; enumeration algorithm computes ranges
Remarks: Recompute ranges on hot-plug or topology change; ensure MEMSPACE aligns to BAR sizes of children.

description: General purpose registers provide scratch or board-specific control knobs within the PCIe module. They can be used for firmware handshaking or debug breadcrumbs during bring-up. Access is via standard memory-mapped interface in the application register space. These registers have no side effects unless firmware assigns semantics. They are useful to trace init phases or store magic values for crash triage.
dependencies: Module accessible; software convention established for meanings
Remarks: Document firmware usage to avoid cross-team conflicts; clear on reset paths if needed.

description: The controller’s application/configuration register space is organized with fixed offsets independent of access mode. A 1 KB configuration space is described with stable register offsets. This consistency simplifies access whether viewed from internal or external buses. Software can rely on the documented offsets during both RC and EP operation. Stable layout eases driver portability across modes.
evidence: Statement: "1 KB space of the PCIe configuration space. The offsets of the registers do not change irrespective of the access mode." (Chapter 2/3 context)
dependencies: Base address mapped; correct endianness handling in software
Remarks: Use symbolic offsets from headers to avoid hardcoding; verify window mapping after reset.

description: The SerDes block includes per-lane configuration for at least two lanes, enabling x1 or x2 link widths. Lane-specific RX settings (e.g., RX_LOS) are configured per-lane during test or bring-up. Two-lane mode requires configuring both Lane 0 and Lane 1 before initiating training. This provides bandwidth scaling based on board routing and device support. Software must coordinate width negotiation with the far-end partner.
evidence: Procedure note referencing SerDes Configuration Lane 0/1 and two-lane mode in loopback setup (Section around 2.11.1.1/SerDes)
dependencies: Board supports two lanes; both lanes configured; link partner capable of x2
Remarks: Validate lane polarity and equalization per board; fall back to x1 if link partners or routing limit width.
